title: Leadership in the AI Era: 5 Habits That Still Matter
excerpt: AI changes the tools, not the fundamentals. Here are five habits every leader needs when machines do the busywork.
body:
## Why leadership still matters

AI can write the report, summarize the meeting, and draft the plan. What it cannot do is own the outcome, build trust, or take the blame when things go wrong.

### 1. Decide with incomplete information

Waiting for perfect data is a luxury you rarely have. Train your team to act on 70% and adjust.

### 2. Protect focus

Tools multiply output but also noise. The leader's job is to kill the noise.

### 3. Give credit, take blame

Public praise, private correction, personal ownership of failure. This never goes out of style.

### 4. Teach as you go

A leader who hoards context creates a bottleneck. Share the 'why' with every 'what'.

### 5. Stay calm under ambiguity

Markets shift, models break, plans slip. Your calm is the team's anchor.

> The machine optimizes the task. You optimize the mission.

title: Write Content That Sells (Without Sounding Salesy)
excerpt: The best content teaches first and sells second. A practical framework for turning readers into buyers.
body:
## Teach, then sell

Readers trust people who help before they ask. Lead with a useful idea; the pitch comes after.

### The 3-part post

1. **Hook** — name a specific problem the reader feels.
2. **Value** — give one actionable insight they can use today.
3. **Bridge** — show how your product takes them further.

### Voice rules

- Short sentences.
- One idea per paragraph.
- No jargon unless you explain it.

> Sell the outcome, not the feature.

title: Negotiate Without Losing Trust: 4 Moves
excerpt: A good deal is one both sides want to repeat. Four moves that get you the price and keep the relationship.
body:
## The deal is the start, not the end

Win the price and lose the relationship and you've lost. Here's how to win both.

### 1. Anchor with fairness, not fear

Open with a number that's ambitious but defensible. Extreme anchors signal you don't trust them.

### 2. Trade, don't concede

Every 'yes' to them costs you something — get something back. Concessions given free are worth nothing.

### 3. Name the invisible issue

The real blocker is rarely price. It's risk, timing, or ego. Say it out loud.

### 4. Close with a one-line summary

'So we agree: X for Y, by Z.' Written in one sentence, it removes 80% of future disputes.

> Trust is the only currency that compounds.

title: A Deep Productivity System That Survives Real Life
excerpt: Most systems break on the first busy week. Build one that bends instead of snapping.
body:
## Systems beat willpower

Willpower is a battery. A system is a pipeline. Design for the day you're tired.

### The 3-bucket rule

1. **Deep** — one big task, mornings, phone away.
2. **Shallow** — replies, admin, in a single time block.
3. **Zero** — one full day with no work at all.

### The 2-minute gate

If it takes under 2 minutes, do it now. If not, it goes to a bucket — never your head.

### The bounce-back rule

Miss a day? Don't miss twice. The second miss is a habit; the first is an accident.

> A system that needs a perfect day to work is not a system.

title: Invest Like an Owner, Not a Gambler
excerpt: The market rewards patience and punishes noise. A calm framework for long-term wealth.
body:
## Own, don't bet

A stock is a slice of a business. Treat it like buying a company, not a lottery ticket.

### The 3 questions before any buy

1. Do I understand how it makes money?
2. Will this matter in 10 years?
3. Am I buying at a price with a margin of safety?

### The noise tax

Checking prices hourly costs you returns. Every trade is a fee; every emotion is a tax.

### The auto-pilot

Set a fixed amount, on a fixed day, no matter the headline. Time in the market beats timing it.

> Wealth is what you keep, not what you chase.

title: Tech for Non-Techies: Speak the Language
excerpt: You don't need to code to lead in tech. Learn the 10 terms that unlock every conversation.
body:
## The 10 words that unlock tech

You don't need to write code to be fluent in tech. You need to understand the map.

### 1. API

The waiters between softwares — they take a request and bring data back.

### 2. Database

Where everything is stored. Think library, not spreadsheet.

### 3. Cloud

Someone else's computer, rented by the hour.

### 4. Deploy

Shipping the code to real users.

### 5. Latency

The delay you feel. Lower is better.

### 6-10. Stack, cache, bug, CI/CD, open-source

Each is a lever. Learn the lever, not the wiring.

> Fluency is knowing what to ask, not how to build.

title: Health Is a System, Not a Willpower Contest
excerpt: You won't out-discipline a bad environment. Redesign the system and the body follows.
body:
## Design out the friction

Most health plans fail on week two because they rely on daily heroics. Systems don't.

### The 3 defaults

1. **Sleep anchor** — fixed bedtime, same every night. Energy compounder #1.
2. **One plate rule** — half vegetables, no second helping in the kitchen.
3. **Movement snacks** — 3 minutes every hour beats one gym marathon.

### The environment hack

Don't buy the snack, don't debate it. Remove the choice, remove the failure.

> A healthy default beats a strong intention every single day.

title: Languages in 90 Days: A Realistic Plan
excerpt: Fluency is a myth for beginners; usable is the goal. A 90-day path to speaking.
body:
## Usable, not perfect

Chase conversations, not certificates. Here's the 90-day shape.

### Days 1-30: Sounds + 500 words

Own the pronunciation early. Words without sound are silent.

### Days 31-60: Sentence patterns

Learn 20 frames ('I want to __', 'Where is __'). Fill the blank live.

### Days 61-90: Talk to humans

Language exchanges, apps, neighbors. Embarrassment is tuition.

### The daily 20

Twenty minutes daily beats a Sunday binge. Consistency is the cheat code.

> You don't learn a language. You live one, a little, every day.
